questions.question: How does Rust achieve encapsulation?
options: Through the `pub` keyword — struct fields are private by default
Using getters generated by the compiler
questions.explanation: Rust's privacy system provides encapsulation. Struct fields default to private; only the module that defines them can access them directly.

questions.question: Rust supports traditional class-based inheritance.
questions.explanation: Rust doesn't have class inheritance. Instead, it uses traits for polymorphism and composition over inheritance.

questions.question: Define a function that accepts any type implementing the Draw trait via a trait object:
questions.explanation: `dyn Draw` creates a trait object enabling dynamic dispatch. Box<dyn Draw> can hold any type that implements Draw.

questions.question: What is the difference between static dispatch and dynamic dispatch?
options: Static resolves types at compile time (generics), dynamic resolves at runtime (dyn Trait)
Dynamic dispatch doesn't work with traits
questions.explanation: Generics use static dispatch (monomorphization). Trait objects (`dyn Trait`) use dynamic dispatch via vtables, adding a small runtime cost.

questions.question: A trait can only be used as a trait object if it is 'object-safe' — meaning its methods don't use generics or return Self.
questions.explanation: Object safety requires that the compiler can construct a vtable. Methods returning Self or using generics prevent this because the concrete type is unknown.

questions.question: In the state pattern shown in the chapter, why are states represented as trait objects?
options: So different state types can share a common interface and be swapped at runtime
questions.explanation: Trait objects allow different state structs (Draft, PendingReview, Published) to implement the same State trait, enabling runtime polymorphism.
